coredata.dc:description: Organic peroxides in foodstuffs like lipid hydroperoxides and benzoyl peroxide (BPO) have risks to human health, which need rapid and on-site determination. This study prepared hydrophobic and reductive fluorescent iodine-doping carbon dots (I-CDs) via a solvothermal strategy using oleylammonium iodide as the precursor. The I-CDs can be used as nanoprobes to rapidly and sensitively determine organic peroxides. Organic peroxides can oxidize the I-CDs to produce I2, which can efficiently quench their fluorescence through the inner filter effect. Furthermore, hydrophobic aerogels were prepared to incorporate the I-CDs, constructing a fluorescent sensor. When combined with a smartphone and portable detection equipment, the developed sensor enables rapid on-site testing of peroxide values (PVs) in edible oils, as well as BPO in flour. The fluorescent sensor has a detection limit of 1.2 mmol/kg for PVs and 0.10 mmol/kg for BPO. Our study provides an efficient tool for monitoring organic peroxides in foodstuffs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of I-CDs. (B) UV–vis spectra of I-CDs and oleylamine and mixture of oleylamine and I2. The inset pictures show precursor (left) and I-CDs (right) in n-butanol solution. (C) Two-dimensional fluorescence plot of I-CDs solution. (D) QY spectra of I-CDs n-butanol solution and reference.

(A) TEM image of I-CDs. The inset graph shows the diameter distribution statistical chart of I-CDs. (B) Two-dimension AFM image of I-CDs. (C) Three-dimensional AFM morphology of I-CDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of I-CDs. (E) XPS survey spectrum of I-CDs. (F) High-resolution I 3d XPS spectra of I-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs, I-CDs/CHP, and I-CDs/BPO in n-butanol. The inset image shows a fluorescence change in the I-CDs solution reaction to organic peroxides. The concentration of I-CDs was 0.5 mg/mL. (B) UV–vis spectra of I2, I-CDs, CHP, BPO, I-CDs/CHP mixture, and I-CDs/BPO in n-butanol. (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of I-CDs and I-CDs/I2; UV–vis absorption spectra of I2 n-butanol solutions. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of I-CDs, I-CDs/CHP, I-CDs/BPO and I-CDs/I2 mixture in n-butanol. (E) Scheme of the proposed sensing quenching mechanisms of I-CDs towards organic peroxide.

(A) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity difference (F0 F) and various concentrations of BPO. (B) Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs towards fresh and oxidized edible oils. The inset illustration shows the fluorescence changes of I-CDs solution with fresh (left) and oxidized (right) edible oils. The concentration of I-CDs was 0.5 mg/mL. (C) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity difference (F0 F) and various contents of PVs in edible oils. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the initial I-CDs solution and the quenched solution in the fluorescence assay, respectively. The concentration of I-CDs was 0.5 mg/mL. Error bars represent three independent replicates.

(A) Scheme of the fabrication of the fluorescence aerogel sensor. (B) SEM image of MTMS-modified hydrophobic aerogels. (C) XPS survey spectra of the ALG/CNF aerogels and MTMS-modified ALG/CNF aerogels. (D) High-resolution XPS results of C 1 s spectra of aerogels. (E) High-resolution XPS results of Si 2p spectrum of silane-modified aerogels. (F) The water and edible oil contact angle of MTMS-modified ALG/CNF aerogels. (G) Photo of low bulk density aerogels stands on the top of a dandelion.

(A) Schematic illustration of the on-site detection of organic peroxides using the fluorescence aerogel test kits. (B) Photographs of the vacuum-sealed aerogels in the 48-well plate. Inset images show the morphology of an aerogels under daylight and ultraviolet irradiation. (C) The CIE color coordinates of the fluorescence aerogels. (D) The design of a smartphone-based portable sensing platform for determining organic peroxides. (E) The calibration curve for detecting organic peroxides is plotted using the B value. The contents of lipid peroxides ranged from 0.1 to 32.4 mmol/kg, and the concentration range of BPO was 0–6.0 mmol/kg. The inset image shows fluorescence changes in the aerogel test kits. Error bars represent three independent replicates. (F) The table shows the content of organic peroxides in edible oils and flours using fluorescence aerogel sensor and the titrimetric method.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-functionalized aerogel sensor for fluorescent and portable monitoring of organic peroxides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic peroxides in foodstuffs like lipid hydroperoxides and benzoyl peroxide (BPO) have risks to human health, which need rapid and on-site determination. This study prepared hydrophobic and reductive fluorescent iodine-doping carbon dots (I-CDs) via a solvothermal strategy using oleylammonium iodide as the precursor. The I-CDs can be used as nanoprobes to rapidly and sensitively determine organic peroxides. Organic peroxides can oxidize the I-CDs to produce I2, which can efficiently quench their fluorescence through the inner filter effect. Furthermore, hydrophobic aerogels were prepared to incorporate the I-CDs, constructing a fluorescent sensor. When combined with a smartphone and portable detection equipment, the developed sensor enables rapid on-site testing of peroxide values (PVs) in edible oils, as well as BPO in flour. The fluorescent sensor has a detection limit of 1.2 mmol/kg for PVs and 0.10 mmol/kg for BPO. Our study provides an efficient tool for monitoring organic peroxides in foodstuffs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic peroxides present in food, industrial products, and the environment pose a potential threat to human health. Once ingested through food or other means, organic peroxides can damage tissues and organs; furthermore, some have been identified as having carcinogenic potential (Lee et al., 2023). Therefore, the rapid and sensitive detection of organic peroxides is crucial for protecting human health. Lipid hydroperoxides and benzoyl peroxides (BPO) are typical hydrophobic organic peroxides closely associated with human health. Lipid hydroperoxides are formed through the oxidation of edible oils, while BPO is often illegally added to food products. The lipids in edible oils are particularly vulnerable to oxidation in the presence of elevated temperatures, high oxygen concentrations, intense light, and metal ions (Khabbaz et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Lipid oxidation can lead to the generation and accumulation of hydroperoxides in edible oils. The intake of excessive hydroperoxides can promote diseases in the human body, including arteriosclerosis, myocardial infarction, and other chronic conditions (Ambreen et al., 2020; Ganesan et al., 2018). As a crucial indicator, peroxide value (PV), defined as the millimolar equivalent of peroxides in a kilogram of oil sample, is commonly used to monitor lipid oxidation during oil processing and preservation (Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, many countries have established strict standards for the content of peroxides in edible oils to safeguard human health. According to the Chinese national standard GB/T 2716–2018, the approved PV is less than 9.75 mmol/kg (0.25 g/100 g) (National Health and Family Planning Commission of the People's Republic of China, 2018). Thus, measuring PVs is significant for assessing the quality of edible oils. Iodometric titration is the classical method for determining PVs in edible oils; however, it usually suffers from time-consuming operations, involves the use of toxic solvents, and raises sustainability concerns.

BPO, with its strong oxidation capability, is widely used as a flour whitening agent in the food industry. The accumulation of BPO in the human body can cause allergic reactions and tissue damage (Jia et al., 2011). Excessive addition of BPO in flour can result in the degeneration of essential nutrients. Moreover, BPO can also be converted into hazardous substances such as benzoic acid, biphenyl, and phenyl benzoate, which further increase health risks (Kucera et al., 2024; Olmo et al., 2017). Considering the health risks to the human body, nations and international organizations have established strict regulations regarding the use of BPO. The maximum allowable limits of BPO in flour are set at 50 mg/kg (0.20 mmol/kg) in the USA and 300 mg/kg (1.24 mmol/kg) in Japan (Fu et al., 2020). However, BPO has been prohibited as a food additive in wheat flour by China (Ministry of Health and 7 Other Departments, 2011). Despite this, BPO could still potentially be illegally used to gain extra profits. Therefore, rapid and accurate monitoring of BPO is necessary to ensure food safety and protect human health.

Instrumental analyses such as gas chromatography and liquid chromatography have been established to determine organic peroxides (Liang et al., 2022; Usman & Abbas, 2023). Although these instrumental methods can provide accurate results, they inevitably require expensive instruments, cumbersome pretreatment, and well-trained operators. In contrast, spectroscopic techniques such as infrared spectroscopy and multispectral imaging can enable the rapid detection of PVs and BPO (Kim et al., 2022; Shang et al., 2018). The aforementioned methods usually require considerable data analysis and model construction, while reproducibility and precise quantitative measurement remain challenging. Therefore, further efforts are needed to develop novel strategies for achieving the rapid, facile, and on-site determination of organic peroxides. Recently, the direct and rapid determination of organic peroxides based on nanoprobes (Wu et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2023) has garnered increasing attention from researchers. For instance, oil-soluble fluorescent perovskite nanocrystals (CsPbBr3) have been successfully applied to rapidly and sensitively determine organic peroxides (Zhu et al., 2019). Peroxides can alter the structure of perovskite nanocrystals through oxidation reaction, leading to fluorescence quenching or wavelength shifts. Although perovskite nanocrystals exhibit outstanding optoelectronic properties, such as high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and narrow, tunable emission bands, the environmental issues caused by the leakage of lead in CsPbBr3 have raised concerns (Chen et al., 2023). Furthermore, interfering compounds in edible oils could affect the photoluminescence properties of perovskite nanocrystals, which hinders their further advancement (Zhao et al., 2021). Therefore, there is a continued need to develop green and stable nanoprobes for the determination of organic peroxides.

As an emerging and green material, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn extensive attention in various applications, such as sensor and bio-imaging, due to their superior merits, including good photostability, easy synthesis, and being heavy metal-free (Ðorđević et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Traditional synthesis strategies, including hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods, typically yield water-soluble CDs (Han et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2020), which hinder their sensing applications in oil-phase foods. Recently, several studies have succeeded in synthesizing and applying amphiphilic or oil-soluble CDs with high QY by optimizing the precursors, solvents, and reaction conditions (Gao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Inspired by these studies, we aimed to develop a fluorescence method for the rapid and on-site determination of organic peroxides by using novel oil-soluble CDs in our research. The iodide-doped CDs (I-CDs) were synthesized based on the single-step carbonization of oleylammonium iodide as the precursor. The obtained I-CDs solution was colorless and exhibited strong blue fluorescence. The sensing mechanism of I-CDs was proposed after characterizations. The reaction between I-CDs and organic peroxides can release I2, which efficiently quenches the fluorescence of I-CDs. To achieve the rapid and on-site detection of peroxides, aerogels were used to develop portable sensor. Aerogels are mesoporous gel materials with high specific surface area and low density. Compared with hydrogel and organogel, aerogel contains no liquid solvents, and the surface of aerogels can be facilely modified to get good hydrophobicity. Moreover, aerogel has porous structure which can enable it to densely incorporate the I-CDs. Hydrophobic and porous aerogel can rapidly absorb fat-soluble samples through capillary force. Using alginate (ALG) and cellulose nanofiber (CNF) as raw materials, hydrophobic aerogels loaded with oil-soluble I-CDs were developed to achieve on-site determination of organic peroxides. The proposed method has detection limits of 1.2 mmol/kg for PVs in edible oils and 0.10 mmol/kg for BPO in flour. The entire detection process can be completed within 25 min. Our study offers a new strategy for the on-site monitoring of organic peroxides with quick response, high reliability, and sustainability. Additionally, it extends the application of CDs as “green” nanoprobes in food monitoring.

Oleylamine, iodine (I2), potassium iodide (KI), sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3·5H2O), gluconolactone, cumene hydroperoxide (CHP), and BPO were ordered from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Toluene, n-butanol, chloroform, soluble starch, and acetic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methyltrimethoxysilane (MTMS), calcium carbonate (CaCO3), and sodium alginate were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) were provided by Northern Century Cellulose Materials Co., Ltd. Commercial edible oils were sourced from the local market. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade.

Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrometer (UV − vis, Thermo UV − 10s). Lengguang F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was used to record the fluorescence intensity and spectra. QY and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were carried out in an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrofluorometer. Infrared spectra were characterized on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, PerkinElmer Frontier). The morphology of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher Talos F200S G2) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon). X − ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250) was utilized to determine the elemental composition and chemical state. The portable detection device was manufactured using a three-dimensional printer (Creality, Ender-3 S1 Plus). All photographs were taken with a smartphone camera (iPhone 8 pro).

The I-CDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method. Briefly, 1.0 g of iodine was added to 4.5 g of oleylamine in a glass tube. The mixture was uniformly mixed by ultrasound. The mixture was then heated in a high-temperature oven at 160 °C for at least 2 h to obtain crude I-CDs product. The I-CDs were purified through silica gel column chromatography with dichloromethane as the eluent, followed by evaporating the solvent under reduced pressure. The purified products were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further characterization. I-CDs powder is directly used for XPS and FTIR testing. The integral sphere method measures the QY of I-CDs dissolved in n-butanol solution. The I-CDs n-butanol solutions were deposited onto mica flakes and copper mesh for AFM and TEM characterization.

According to the previous literature (Yang et al., 2021), the preparation procedure of the aerogels was as follows: First, 2.0 mL of CNFs (10 %, w/v) was combined with 9.0 mL of ALG solution (2 %, w/v) and homogenized under continuous stirring to form a stable suspension. Next, 4.5 mL of gluconolactone (1 %, w/v) and 4.5 mL of CaCO3 (0.5 %, w/v) were introduced into the suspension and mixed thoroughly to achieve a uniform dispersion. After that, the 0.25 mL of the above solution was transported into individual wells of an assay plate. The solutions were then allowed to undergo gelation at room temperature, yielding transparent hydrogels. The plate was placed in contact with a thermally conductive plate submerged in liquid nitrogen to freeze the hydrogels, followed by freeze-drying for 48 h to yield ALG/CNFs aerogels. The aerogels were hydrophobically with MTMS using chemical vapor deposition. Briefly, 2.0 mL of MTMS and 2.0 mL of distilled water were placed in separate vials, and the unmodified aerogels into an airtight container and place them in an oven at 70 °C for a silanization reaction of 4 h. Subsequently, the modified aerogels were removed and transferred to a vacuum oven at 25 °C for 24 h to remove excess MTMS. This yielded the final MTMS-modified aerogels. A hydrophobic aerogel was soaked in 0.3 mL of I-CDs dichloromethane solution (2 mg/mL) and dried in a vacuum to obtain fluorescence aerogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PVs using aerogel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 0.1 g of peanut oil or soybean oil samples was mixed with 1.0 mL of n-butanol for the aerogel sensing system. As for flour samples, 0.5 g was added to 1.0 mL of n-butanol for extraction. The resulting solutions were dripped directly onto the fluorescence aerogels. After 20 min incubation, a portable smartphone-based detection device was used to quantify fluorescence intensity. Images of the aerogels were captured under UV light irradiation using the portable device. Image analysis software, Colorimetric Titration (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/colorimetric-titration/id1533793244), was used to obtain RGB values.

For the solvent detection system, The I-CDs were diluted to 0.5 mg/mL and 5.0 mg/mL for PVs and BPO detection, respectively. 0.1 g of edible oil or 0.1 mL of CHP standard solutions was added to the 2.0 mL of I-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). As for BPO quantitative analysis, 0.1 mL of I-CDs solutions (5.0 mg/mL) was added into 0.9 mL of sample extractions or standard solutions. After incubated 15 min, the fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) and fluorescence intensity (λex = 365 nm, λem = 440 nm) of the mixture were recorded. The excitation and emission bandwidths were both 10 nm.

The commercial edible oils were heated at 60 °C for 40 days to obtain oxidized edible oils with high PVs. The oxidized oils were mixed with fresh edible oils at various ratios (ranging from 1:9 to 7:3) to obtain standard edible oil with different PVs. The PVs and BPO contents of these edible oil and flour samples were measured using the Na2S2O3-based titration method according to the National Test Standard of China (GB/T 5009.227–2023) (National Health and Family Planning Commission of the People's Republic of China, 2023). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated by 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and S is the slope of the regression line.

Currently, most reported CDs are hydrophilic and can only be well dispersed in water and polar solvents. There is significant importance and growing interest in synthesizing hydrophobic CDs to meet the requirements for analyzing fatty foods. In this study, we synthesized hydrophobic I-CDs through solvothermal method (Liu et al., 2024) using oleylamine and iodine as precursors (Fig. 1A). First, I2 reacted with oleylamine to form the oleylammonium iodide under heated conditions (Tran et al., 2022). Subsequently, the hydrophobic I-CDs were obtained by carbonizing the oleylammonium iodide precursor. A light yellow-colored solution of I-CDs was obtained after the completion of carbonization (Inset of Fig. 1B). The UV–vis absorption spectra of I-CDs and precursor solution are shown in Fig. 1B. The iodine solution showed strong and characteristic absorption peaks at 293 nm and 360 nm, respectively. The I-CDs solution showed slight absorption in the UV region, with no prominent absorption peak. High transparency of the I-CDs solution is beneficial for improving the analytical sensitivity due to reducing the nonspecific absorption caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) (Ye, Ren, et al., 2023; Baheti & Vigalok, 2019). Several characterizations were performed to reveal the properties of the I-CDs. As shown in two-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1C). The maximum emission peak of I-CDs is located at 440 nm under an excitation of 365 nm. The I-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission, consistent with the typical CDs in previous reports (LeCroy et al., 2017). The effect of temperature and time on the hydrothermal synthesis of I-CDs was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1A, the reaction time was fixed at 2 h, and the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs increased as the reaction temperature rose until it reached saturation at 160 °C. At this optimal temperature, extending the hydrothermal time further enhanced the fluorescence intensity, and the intensity gradually reached its maximum after 4 h (Fig. S1B). Therefore, a temperature of 160 °C and reaction time of 4 h were selected as the optimal hydrothermal conditions for synthesizing I-CDs. The I-CDs exhibited oleophilic solubility due to the abundant hydrophobic oleylamine ligands on their surface. They could be well dispersed in various non-polar solvents, including n-butanol, toluene, hexane, et al. (Fig. S2A). As displayed in Fig. S2B, the I-CDs displayed the strongest fluorescence intensity in n-butanol. According to the previous research, the solvent effect significantly influences the fluorescence properties of CDs (Gao et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). As a protic solvent with low polarity, n-butanol forms hydrogen bonds with the I-CDs, which is the primary reason for the fluorescence enhancement of the I-CDs (Bai et al., 2019). The QY of the I-CDs was determined to be 25.4 % in n-butanol using an integration sphere method (Fig. 1D). The relatively high QY is beneficial for establishing a highly sensitive fluorescence method (Han et al., 2020). Non-doped CDs were synthesized using oleylamine as the precursor under the same reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, the solution of non-doped CDs exhibits almost no fluorescence compared to the I-CDs. This is because oleylamine iodide is more easily oxidized and undergoes carbonization and polymerization compared to oleylamine. Additionally, iodine ions can facilitate the carbonization process of the precursor, thereby promoting the formation of I-CDs (Wang et al., 2024).

Synthesizing various types of nanomaterials in organic solvents rather than aqueous solutions is a crucial strategy for broadening their practical applications. The choice of non-aqueous solvents plays a pivotal role in influencing the performance of the resulting hydrophobic nanomaterials. Oleylamine, a long-chain primary alkylamine, has a relatively low melting point (∼20 °C) and high boiling point (∼350 °C). Additionally, oleylamine possesses mild reducibility and good coordination capability. Consequently, oleylamine serves as a versatile reagent for synthesizing hydrophobic nanoparticles, including CDs (Mourdikoudis & Liz-Marzán, 2013; Zhang et al., 2024). This study synthesized oleylammonium iodide through a redox reaction between I2 and oleylamine (Tran et al., 2022). We utilized oleylammonium iodide as the precursor to synthesize I-CDs via a one-step solvothermal method. The resulting I-CDs exhibited good hydrophobicity and high QY, showing significant potential for applications in analysis of non-aqueous foods such as edible oils.

Several characterizations were performed to reveal the structural properties of the I-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, nanoscale carbon nanoparticles can be observed, indicating the successful synthesis of I-CDs. TEM analysis revealed that the I-CDs exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution and had an average diameter of approximately 4.6 nm. AFM images also indicated that the I-CDs are monodispersed and have a uniform distribution in heights (approximately 4.6 nm) (Fig. 2B and Fig. 2C), which is in good agreement with the TEM results. The surface functional groups of I-CDs were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2D, the NH stretching vibration was observed as a broad peak around 3451 cm−1. Aliphatic CH stretching was identified by peaks at 2800–3000 cm−1, and a C=C–H stretching peak appeared at 3005 cm−1. Additionally, there were peaks for NH bending at 1625 cm−1 and methylene bending vibration at 1467 cm−1. The FTIR results preliminarily confirmed the capping of oleylamine on the I-CDs. XPS spectra were measured to characterize the chemical composition of the I-CDs. The results of the survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 2E showed three main signal peaks derived from the I, C, and N elements (I 3d, C 1 s, and N 1 s). This confirms that the iodine element has been doped into the CDs. The high-resolution and deconvoluted XPS spectrum of the iodine element in the I-CDs is shown in Fig. 2F. Due to the spin-orbit splitting in the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 bonds, the two major peaks attributed to I− appeared at 617.7 eV and 629.2 eV, while the two minor peaks of I2 appeared at 619.5 eV and 631.0 eV. These results indicated that the iodine element mainly exists in the form of I− in the I-CDs. The two peaks at 284.8 eV and 286.0 eV in the high-resolution C 1 s spectra correspond to the CC and CN bonds, respectively (Fig. S4A). The N 1 s spectra in Fig. S4B showed two peaks at 400.6 eV and 401.5 eV, which are assigned to C–NH2 and charge-transfer complexes between I2 and –NH2, respectively (Guo et al., 2023). The XPS results indicated that the iodine element was successfully doped into the I-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of organic peroxides in solvent using I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our study, we hypothesized that the synthesized I-CDs could be oxidized and quenched by organic peroxides, leading to changes in fluorescence intensity. We investigated the effects of CHP and BPO, both typical oil-soluble peroxides, on the fluorescence of I-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of the I-CDs had an obvious reduction after the introduction of CHP and BPO. The quenching ratio of I-CDs exceeded 90 % when the final concentration of CHP and BPO reaches about 4 mmol/kg. These results suggest that the I-CDs have the potential to serve as a sensitive fluorescent probe for organic peroxides.

The quenching mechanism for I-CDs induced by organic peroxides was further studied. As displayed in Fig. 3B, after the redox reaction, the mixture of I-CDs and typical oil-soluble peroxides (CHP or BPO) exhibited two emerging absorption peaks at 293 nm and 360 nm, corresponding to the characteristic absorption of I2. The change in absorption indicated the generation of I2, which subsequently led to the fluorescence quenching of I-CDs. The interaction between the fluorescence spectra of I-CDs and the absorption spectra of I2 is presented in Fig. 3C. The maximum fluorescence excitation peak of I-CDs is located at 365 nm, close to the main absorption peak of I2 (λ = 360 nm). The absorption spectra of I2 significantly overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of I-CDs. Previous studies have demonstrated that I2 possesses a high molar-extinction coefficient and strong fluorescence quenching capacity (Ye, Yu, et al., 2023). The excitation light for the I-CDs in photoluminescence can be attenuated by I2, resulting in a decrease in their emission intensity. Upon the addition of I2 to the I-CD solution, a substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed in both the excitation and emission spectra. Furthermore, the starch indicator experiment depicted in Fig. S5A indicates that the reaction between CHP and I-CDs results in the liberation of I2. Therefore, we theorized that the IFE should be the dominant reason for the fluorescence quenching of the I-CDs. To further elucidate the quenching mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3D, the average fluorescence lifetime (τ0) of I-CDs was determined to be 2.36 ns, and the introduction of I2 into I-CDs hardly affects their fluorescence lifetime (τI2=2.38 ns). Analogous outcomes were observed upon introducing 2 mmol/kg of CHP and BPO into the I-CDs solution, with fluorescence lifetimes of τCHP = 2.40 ns and τBPO = 2.27 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes of I-CDs before and after quenching exhibited no significant variation, suggesting the absence of energy transfer or interaction between I-CDs and the quencher (Tropp et al., 2019). Therefore, based on the spectral and fluorescence lifetime results, we inferred that the IFE is the primary mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching in the I-CDs (Chen et al., 2022). The proposed mechanism of peroxide-triggered fluorescence quenching of the I-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 3E. The I-CDs exhibited strong reducibility and could be oxidized by various organic peroxides to generate I2. The fluorescence of I-CDs was effectively quenched by I2 through the occurrence of IFE.

Current research indicated that reductive CDs can be synthesized by selecting appropriate precursors. For instance, Wang et al. prepared reductive CDs using m-aminophenol as a precursor. The abundant polyaromatic units and phenolic hydroxyl on their surfaces endowed the CDs with strong reducibility, enabling them serve as sensitive probes for the rapid detection of hypochlorous acid (Wang et al., 2020). However, most of the reported reductive CDs were hydrophilic, which limits their application in nonaqueous circumstances like edible oils. Previous studies have also shown that heteroatom doping is an effective strategy for tuning the optical and chemical properties of CDs by modifying their structures (Ansari et al., 2021; Miao et al., 2020). In this study, we successfully synthesized the I-CDs using oleylammonium iodide as the precursor, demonstrating that iodine doping is an effective strategy for preparing hydrophobic and reductive CDs. The quenching kinetics of I-CDs induced by CHP were recorded and are shown in Fig. S5B. The fluorescence intensity of the I-CDs and CHP mixture gradually decreased upon incubation at room temperature (25 °C), stabilizing after 20 min. The fluorescence emission spectra of I-CDs solution containing varying concentrations of CHP were measured. The fluorescence intensities at 440 nm decrease as the CHP concentration increased from 0 to 40 mmol/kg. (Fig. S6A). The standard curve is displayed in Fig. S6B. showing a linear relationship between CHP concentration and fluorescence intensity. The LOD for CHP was calculated to be 0.5 mmol/kg (3σ/S). with a linear regression equation of Y = − 216.96C + 9454.59 (R2 = 0.993). For the detection of illegal added BPO in flour, the mixing ratio of I-CDs and sample solution was adjusted. I-CDs were mixed with various concentrations of BPO for 20 min, and the fluorescence difference (F0 F) before and after the reaction was recorded. The calibration curve between BPO concentration and F0 F values is shown in Fig. 4A, with a linear regression equation of ΔF = 4948.25C – 33.52 (R2 = 0.9934). Based on this calibration curve, the LOD for BPO was determined to be 0.05 mmol/kg (3σ/S), which is lower than the regulatory limits set by various countries (Fu et al., 2020). For countries where BPO use is prohibited, this method can serve as a rapid screening tool for detecting its presence.

Prior to actual samples analysis, the selectivity of the I-CDs was evaluated by testing common compounds found in edible oils and flour samples, including glycerol, stearic acid, palmitic acid, oleic acid, linoleic acid, linolenic acid, vitamin B2, β-sitosterol, β-carotene and vitamin E. As illustrated in Fig. S7A, the introduction of these compounds did not significantly alter the fluorescence intensity of the I-CDs. This result demonstrates that the I-CDs exhibit selective quenching behavior exclusively in the presence of organic peroxides, highlighting their specificity for peroxide detection. Edible oil samples with high PVs were prepared by accelerated aging treatment, involving storage at 60 °C for 40 days. The standard titration method measured the accurate PV of edible oil to be 32.4 mmol/kg. As shown in the spectral results in Fig. 4B, fresh peanut oil did not significantly affect the fluorescence of the I-CDs, whereas oxidized peanut oil with a high PV markedly quenched the fluorescence of the I-CDs. These results confirm that the I-CDs can rapidly detect PV in edible oils. We further optimized the detection conditions, including temperature and reaction time. As illustrated in Fig. S7B, the kinetics of oils-induced quenching of I-CDs at 25 °C (room temperature), 35 °C, and 45 °C indicated that the decline in fluorescence intensity stabilizes after 20 min for all three conditions. This suggests that increasing the temperature has a minimal effect on promoting fluorescence quenching. Therefore, a reaction time of 20 min at room temperature was selected as the optimal detection condition.

To create a series of oil samples with varying PVs, oxidized edible oil was mixed with different proportions of fresh peanut oil. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs gradually decreased with the increase in PVs in peanut oil. A strong linear correlation was observed between the change in fluorescence intensity of the I-CDs and the PVs. The fitted standard curve was ΔF = 197.65C – 19.54 (R2 = 0.991). Based on this standard curve, the LOD for PV was calculated to be 0.7 mmol/kg (3σ/S), which fully complies with the Chinese National Standard and Codex Alimentarius Commission Standard for PV limits in edible oils (Codex Alimentarius Commission, 1999). These findings demonstrate that our synthesized hydrophobic CDs, which respond to PV in edible oils, have great potential for rapid quality assessment of edible oils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid determination of the organic peroxides using fluorescent aerogel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The developing portable fluorescence sensor enables rapid on-site analysis of food samples, which is an important measure to ensure food safety (Duan et al., 2021). In this study, we developed portable and cost-effective aerogel sensor for the rapid determination of PVs in edible oils and BPO contents in flour samples. The fabrication of the fluorescence aerogel sensor is shown in Fig. 5A. Taking advantage of the excellent and gentle gelation of ALG and the advantage of mechanical properties and high porosity of CNFs, we first prepared a robust ALG/CNFs composite hydrogel (Chen et al., 2021). After freeze-drying treatment, the ALG/CNFs aerogels were obtained. Due to abundant hydrophilic groups like –OH on the aerogels, they exhibited poor lipophilicity. To address this, silane modification was conducted to create a hydrophobic interface (Zhang et al., 2022). Owing to the mild reaction conditions, silane modification was facilely achieved by chemical vapor deposition of methyltrimethoxysilane (MTMS) onto the aerogels. The high volatility of MTMS facilitated its diffusion into the aerogels. The formation mechanism of the modification is schematically shown in Fig. S8. MTMS can be hydrolyzed by water to generate an intermediate product, which subsequently reacts with the hydroxyl groups on the aerogel surface. As a result, the covalent coupling of MTMS on the aerogel surface successfully converts it from hydrophilic to hydrophobic. The morphology and internal microstructure of the aerogels were characterized using SEM. As displayed in Fig. S9, the blank ALG/CNFs aerogels had a typical foam-like porous structure due to the cross-linking of polymers. The MTMS-modified hydrophobic aerogels exhibits a similar porous structure in Fig. 5B, indicating that the silane modification had minimal impact on the porosity of the aerogels. The XPS survey spectra were utilized to determine the chemical composition of the hydrophobic aerogels. The full region XPS spectra in Fig. 5C indicated the existence of Si, C, and O elements (Si 2 s, Si 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively). High-resolution spectra of the C 1 s peak are shown in Fig. 5D. Three peaks at 284.8 eV (CC bonds), 286.2 eV (CO bonds), and 287.7 eV (CO bonds) were observed in the unmodified aerogels. An additional peak at 284.1 eV attributed to the CSi bond was obtained in the MTMS-modified aerogels (Taniyama & Takagi, 2022). In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Si 2p shows a peak belonging to organosilicon chemical bonds at 102.2 eV (Fig. 5E). The XPS results confirm the successful silane modification on aerogels. The water contact angles were measured to confirm the hydrophobicity of the MTMS-modified aerogels. As shown in Fig. 5F, the modified aerogels had a high-water contact angle of 141°, and the contact angle of edible oil was 10°. The result confirmed that silane modification can endow the surface of materials with excellent hydrophobicity and lipophilicity (Zhang et al., 2022). The hydrophobic ALG/CNFs aerogels had good absorption capacity for oils and non-polar solvents due to the plentiful interior pore spaces in the aerogel. The dichloromethane solution of I-CDs was absorbed inside the aerogels. After evaporating the solvent, the I-CDs were dispersed and loaded within the aerogels. The I-CDs functionalized fluorescent aerogels has low bulk density due to its porous structure, and it can stand on the top of a dandelion (Fig. 5G). Aerogels are three-dimensional solid networks that contain no liquid solvents. Therefore, surface modification is an effective strategy for adjusting the hydrophobicity or hydrophilicity of aerogels. In this study, we achieved hydrophobic modification of aerogels using a combination of vapor deposition and silanization, which offers the advantages of simplicity and low cost (Qin et al., 2021). Furthermore, we exploited the porous structure of aerogels to load the I-CDs into the hydrophobic aerogel, demonstrating that aerogels are robust materials for constructing hydrophobic fluorescent sensor.−

We developed a portable sensor based on composited aerogels for on-site PV detection, streamlining the process and reducing the technical expertise required for operators. For large-scale fabrication, we used a commercial 48-well microplate to manufacture the portable sensor kits (Fig. 6A). Each well of microplate was filled with 0.25 mL of ALG/CNFs hydrogel. After freeze-drying, the I-CDs were loaded into the aerogels to obtain the portable kits (Fig. 6B). In order to prove that the carbon dots are successfully loaded onto the aerogel, we conducted XPS analysis of the modified aerogel. As shown in Fig. S10, XPS spectra showed five main signal peaks derived from the I, C, O and Si elements (I 3d, O 1 s, C 1 s, Si 2 s, and Si 2p). The present of iodine element proves that the I-CDs are successfully loaded onto the aerogel. Additionally, we present the fluorescence property of the cross-section of the aerogel sensor under UV light. It shows that the fluorescence inside the aerogel sensor does not significantly differ from that on the surface (Fig. S11A). This indicates that I-CDs are well-loaded within the aerogels.

The stability evaluation of the sensor kits is presented in Fig. S11B. The fluorescence intensities of the composite aerogels remained stable after two months of storage under room temperature and lightproof conditions. The result indicated that the sensor kits can be stored for extended periods after being vacuum-sealed. Upon exposure to BPO and aged edible oils, the composite aerogels immediately absorbed the solutions, accompanied by fluorescence quenching. The corresponding Commission Internationale de l'éclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates stayed in the blue area (Fig. 6C). For achieve the on-site detection, we captured the RGB values from the photographs using a smartphone-based portable sensing platform and a smartphone APP (Fig. 6D) (Liu et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. S12, the B values were selected as the readout signal. The B values of the composite aerogels decreased with incubation time and stabilized within 20 min at room temperature. This indicates that composite aerogels respond rapidly to peroxides in edible oils. The B values of the photographs declined as the PVs in edible oils increased. The fitting curve between B values and PVs is shown in Fig. 6E, and the linear equation was Y = −5.30C + 230.08 (R2 = 0.990). The LOD of PV is calculated to be 1.2 mmol/kg. Additionally, the calibration curve for determining BPO in flour samples was Y = −36.82C + 224.96 (R2 = 0.991), with an LOD of 0.05 mmol/kg. These results demonstrate that the fluorescent aerogel sensor is fully capable of screening organic peroxides exceeding regulatory limits in food samples (National Health and Family Planning Commission of the People's Republic of China, 2018; Fu et al., 2020).

We utilized the aerogel sensor to detect various actual samples and compared the results with those from the standard titrimetric method to assess the suitability and feasibility of the sensor for practical applications. This study analyzed edible oil and flour sourced from local markets. The peroxide content of all commercial samples was within regulatory limits. After accelerating the aging of edible oils and adding high doses of BPO to flour, the obtained peroxide content of samples exceeds the permissible limit. These changes were reflected in significant variations in the B values observed in the aerogel sensor when exposed to different samples. As shown in Fig. 6F, the PVs (peanut oil and soybean oil) and BPO contents (flour) determined by the aerogel sensor were in close agreement with those obtained by the standard titrimetric method. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S13, the R2 value of the test data obtained from the two methods through correlation analysis was 0.994, indicating that the developed aerogel sensor achieved relatively high accuracy in actual samples detection. We reviewed several reported methods for detection of organic peroxides in the last few years (Table S1). Compared with chromatography methods and titration methods, aerogel sensor has advantages of good convenience and detection efficiency. Although the sensitivity of aerogel sensor is not significantly better than several fluorescent methods, aerogel sensor can realize rapid and on-site screening of samples.

In summary, hydrophobic I-CDs were synthesized through a solvothermal strategy using oleylammonium iodide as the precursor. The iodine element in the I-CDs primarily exists in the form of I−, which imparts good reducibility to the I-CDs. Our study indicated that iodine-doping is an efficient strategy for synthesizing hydrophobic and reductive CDs. Organic peroxides can oxidize the I-CDs to produce I2, which quenches their fluorescence through the IFE. The ALG/CNF aerogels prepared by freeze-drying exhibit a porous structure and good stability. After hydrophobic treatment, the aerogels can serve as carriers for the I-CDs. When combined with a smartphone and portable detection equipment, the fluorescent aerogel sensor was successfully used for rapid on-site detection of PVs and BPO. The LODs of the fluorescence sensor for PVs and BPO were 1.2 mmol/kg and 0.10 mmol/kg, respectively. The developed fluorescent sensor is low-cost, portable, and versatile, and it has been successfully applied for monitoring organic peroxides in food samples. Compared with fluorescence spectrophotometers, the smartphone-based portable detection device still exhibits some shortcomings in signal intensity. The sensitivity of the aerogel sensor should be improved in follow-up studies.

Shuai Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Kaiying Hu: Software, Data curation. Lili Yao: Software, Data curation. Yu Mao: Software, Resources, Methodology. Hao Qu: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Baolei Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Lei Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.
